summary: Survey benchmarks used by comparable judge/reward-hacking/self-evolve/agent-eval papers; assess fit for CAGE requirements vs tau2-bench

benchmarks.name: tau2-bench (τ²-bench) / τ-bench
benchmarks.id_or_url: arXiv 2506.07982 (τ²) ; OpenReview roNSXZpUDN (τ¹) ; github.com/sierra-research/tau2-bench ; verified fork github.com/amazon-agi/tau2-bench-verified
benchmarks.size_and_domains: Per-domain task counts: retail 115, airline 50, telecom 114 (plus a 'mock' domain). Each domain = policy doc + tool set + DB. Customer-service dual-control (τ² telecom) and single-control (retail/airline) settings.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: YES — canonical multi-turn tool-use: agent calls DB/API tools across a dialogue with an LLM user-simulator; τ² telecom is dual-control (both user and agent call tools).
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: YES — deterministic outcome verifier compares final DB state to an annotated goal state (plus required-action/comms checks); reliability reported with pass^k. This is exactly the 'hidden programmatic anchor truth' independent of any LLM judge.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: RICH and DOCUMENTED. 2606.09863 measured 9,876 τ² trajectories: 45–48% of failures in single-control domains are 'false success' (agent asserts completion, DB state disagrees); LLM judges never exceed AUROC 0.65, leaning on 'confident closing' language. Ideal attack surface. (Note: dual-control telecom had only ~3% false-success — harder surface.)
benchmarks.used_by: The planned CAGE choice. Used by the false-success paper (2606.09863), the transaction-agent judge blind-spot paper (2606.10315), MUA-RL (2508.18669), and many tool-use RL/agent papers. CAVEAT for req-4: a 54/40/20 = 114-task split works for retail (115) and telecom (114) but airline (50) is far too small; use retail+telecom, or supplement airline. Consider the -verified / -revised forks (they fix mislabeled goal states).

benchmarks.name: AppWorld
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 750 autonomous tasks over 9 day-to-day apps (457 APIs), ~100 simulated users; tasks span 1–4 apps, often 15+ API calls and 80+ lines of code. Split into 'normal' and 'challenge'. Domains = the 9 apps / task-scenario groups.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: YES — interactive code-generation agent that issues many API calls across multiple apps over a long horizon; strongest non-τ candidate for a second domain family.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: YES — hidden state-based programmatic unit tests: snapshot DB state before/after the agent runs, assert all expected changes occurred AND no unexpected changes (collateral damage). Deterministic, re-runnable, LLM-judge-independent.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: RICH and DOCUMENTED. 2606.09863 found 75.8% of AppWorld self-assessing coding-agent failures with explicit status claims are false success; judges over API-call traces reach only AUROC 0.54 (chance), relying on coarse action-volume proxies. Even GPT-4o solves only ~49% normal / ~30% challenge, so plenty of genuine-vs-claimed gaps.
benchmarks.used_by: The false-success paper (2606.09863) as its second environment. Strong recommendation: pair AppWorld with τ² to hit task volume (750) and add a coding-agent false-success surface. Meets all 4 CAGE requirements.

benchmarks.name: AGENTREWARDBENCH (web-agent trajectory eval)
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 1,302 web-agent trajectories drawn from 5 web environments/benchmarks, expert-annotated for task success and side-effects.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: YES — multi-turn web-agent (browser action) trajectories.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: NO (as anchor) — ground truth is human EXPERT annotation. The paper's contribution is showing rule-based programmatic verifiers have high precision but low recall vs experts, and LLM judges over-reward. So it is a judge-reliability dataset, not a re-runnable environment you can drive an agent through.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Yes conceptually — built precisely to expose that automatic evaluations over-credit web-agent trajectories; but the surface is frozen (labeled trajectories), not a live env the CAGE agent can attack.
benchmarks.used_by: Web-agent evaluation and judge-robustness work. Excellent related-work / external judge-eval set for CAGE, but does not itself satisfy req-2 as hidden programmatic anchor truth.

benchmarks.name: AJ-Bench (Agent-as-a-Judge)
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 155 tasks, 516 annotated trajectories across 3 domains: search, data systems, GUI. Binary (positive/negative) labels; judges scored by F1.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: YES — evaluates an 'agent-as-judge' that interacts with environments/tools to verify claims; trajectories are multi-turn tool-use, and judging includes tool-assisted state verification + process verification.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: PARTIAL — the judge agent verifies state via live tool interaction, but the benchmark's ground-truth labels are curated/annotated, not a single deterministic checker. Closest in spirit to CAGE's judge-with-env-access, but not a clean hidden programmatic anchor.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Yes — targets cases where LLM-as-Judge fails to generalize and needs environment evidence to catch unverified claims. Small task count (155) is a limiting factor for 54/40/20 per domain.
benchmarks.used_by: Newer agent-as-judge line. Strong conceptual related work for CAGE's co-evolving judge; task volume too small to be a primary training environment.

benchmarks.name: Claw-Eval
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 300 human-verified tasks, 9 categories in 3 groups (service orchestration, multimodal perception/interaction, multi-turn professional dialogue); 2,159 fine-grained rubric items.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: YES — trajectory-aware, multi-turn; records 3 evidence channels (execution traces, audit logs, environment snapshots); scores Completion/Safety/Robustness with Pass@k and Pass^k.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: PARTIAL — environment snapshots give some deterministic state evidence, but grading is rubric-item based (human/LLM-assisted), not a single hidden programmatic checker. Not a clean anchor-truth verifier.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: RICH conceptually — shows trajectory-opaque (final-output-only) evaluation misses 44% of safety violations and 13% of robustness failures; directly motivates CAGE's thesis. But labels are human-verified, not programmatic.
benchmarks.used_by: Trajectory-aware agent-eval line. Good motivating citation; 300 tasks limits use as a per-domain split source; rubric grading weakens req-2.

benchmarks.name: ToolPRMBench
benchmarks.size_and_domains: Large-scale step-level test cases built on top of several tool-using benchmarks; each case = interaction history + one correct action + one plausible-but-wrong action + tool metadata. Offline sampling (local single-step errors) + online sampling (multi-step failures from full rollouts).
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: PARTIAL — cases are derived from multi-turn tool-use rollouts, but each item is a frozen single-step contrastive choice (correct vs incorrect action), not a live multi-turn environment the agent acts in.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: NO — labels come from a multi-LLM verification pipeline (to reduce noise), not a deterministic environment checker. It is a static PRM-evaluation dataset, not an anchor-truth environment.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Limited — contrastive action pairs, no agent free-form trajectory to fake; not an attack surface in CAGE's sense.
benchmarks.used_by: Process-reward-model literature for tool agents. Relevant related work / candidate external PRM-judge test set; does NOT independently satisfy req-1/req-2 as a training environment.

benchmarks.name: AgentProcessBench
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 1,000 tool-augmented trajectories with 8,509 human-labeled step annotations; ternary labels (correct/helpful, neutral/exploratory, incorrect/harmful); 89.1% inter-annotator agreement.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: YES — realistic tool-augmented multi-turn trajectories; step-level effectiveness task.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: NO — ground truth is HUMAN step annotation, not a programmatic environment verifier. Frozen labeled dataset, not a re-runnable env.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Partial — shows LLMs are biased toward positive labels and can't distinguish neutral exploration from errors (a judge weakness), but the dataset is static, not an env the CAGE agent attacks.
benchmarks.used_by: Step-level agent-evaluation / process-reward line. Good external judge-eval set and related work; human labels mean it is not a hidden programmatic anchor.

benchmarks.name: Agent-RewardBench (multimodal agents)
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 1,136 samples across 3 dimensions (perception, planning, safety) and 7 real-world multimodal agent scenarios (web nav, embodied); step-level reward eval; sampled from 10 models with difficulty control.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: YES (multimodal) — multi-step agent trajectories, but MULTIMODAL (vision-language), which is off-axis from CAGE's text tool-use focus.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: NO — labels are model-sampled + manually verified (human), not a deterministic env checker.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Moderate — step-level reward over agent actions, but static labels; multimodal.
benchmarks.used_by: Multimodal-agent reward-modeling line. Peripheral for CAGE (modality mismatch, human labels).

benchmarks.name: Agent-as-a-Judge / DevAI
benchmarks.size_and_domains: DevAI = 55 realistic AI code-generation tasks with 365 hierarchical solution requirements and rich manual annotations.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: YES — evaluates code-generating agentic systems; judge is itself an agent that inspects intermediate steps.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: NO — ground truth is manual requirement annotations (human); judge reliability measured against human baseline. Not a programmatic env checker.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Some — agent-judge inspects trajectory vs only final output; but only 55 tasks (far too few for 54/40/20 splits) and human labels.
benchmarks.used_by: Foundational 'agent-as-judge' citation. Too small and human-labeled to be a CAGE training environment; cite as motivation.

benchmarks.name: RewardBench / RewardBench 2
benchmarks.size_and_domains: RB2 = 1,865 cases in 6 subsets (Factuality, Focus, Math, Precise Instruction-Following, Safety, Ties); best-of-4 format (1 chosen + 3 rejected).
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: NO — SINGLE-TURN prompt → candidate responses. No tools, no trajectory, no environment. UNFIT for req-1.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: PARTIAL/NO — chosen/rejected preference labels (math subset has objective correctness), but static preference labels, not a re-runnable programmatic environment verifier.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: N/A — no trajectory to fake; it is a preference-ranking task.
benchmarks.used_by: De-facto standard for reward-model papers (Ai2 line, AdaJudge 2601.08097, Beyond Rubrics 2606.07040, and most RM work). For CAGE: include only as a standard external RM sanity-check, NOT a primary benchmark.

benchmarks.name: JudgeBench
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 350 GPT-4o pairs + 270 Claude-3.5-Sonnet pairs across knowledge, reasoning, math, coding; labels derived from reliable verifiers (MMLU-Pro, LiveBench, LiveCodeBench).
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: NO — SINGLE-TURN challenging response-pair comparison. No tool-use, no multi-turn trajectory. UNFIT for req-1.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: PARTIAL — labels are derived from programmatic verifiers on the source datasets, but baked in as static objective-correctness labels; not a live environment checker over a trajectory.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: N/A — pairwise correctness judgment, no agent trajectory to fake.
benchmarks.used_by: Standard hard judge benchmark (strongest judge ~64%). Cite as external judge-quality reference; UNFIT as CAGE training env.

benchmarks.name: RM-Bench
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 1.33k instances across chat, code, math, safety-refuse, safety-response; each prompt has 3 chosen + 3 rejected in 3 styles (concise / detailed plain / detailed markdown).
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: NO — SINGLE-TURN; focus on subtle-content sensitivity and style-bias robustness. No tools/trajectory. UNFIT for req-1.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: NO — static preference labels; no environment verifier.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: N/A — preference task, no trajectory. (Style-bias is a different robustness axis than false-success.)
benchmarks.used_by: Common RM robustness benchmark. Cite for style-bias robustness only; UNFIT as CAGE env.

benchmarks.name: MT-Bench (and MT-Bench-101)
benchmarks.size_and_domains: MT-Bench = 80 two-turn questions, 8 categories, ~3,000 human votes; MT-Bench-101 = 1,388 dialogues / 4,208 turns, 13-task hierarchy.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: NO tool-use — multi-turn CONVERSATION but no tools and no environment. UNFIT for req-1 (multi-turn ≠ tool-use).
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: NO — GPT-4 LLM-as-judge on a 1–10 scale vs human votes. This is the origin of LLM-as-judge, the opposite of a programmatic anchor.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: N/A — no task-completion state; judged on subjective helpfulness. MT-Bench's 80 items are also far too few for splits.
benchmarks.used_by: Ubiquitous chat/judge-bias benchmark (CyclicJudge 2603.01865, etc.). UNFIT for CAGE; only as a legacy judge reference.

benchmarks.name: PandaLM / JudgeLM / Prometheus (fine-tuned judges + their test sets)
benchmarks.id_or_url: PandaLM arXiv 2306.05087 ; JudgeLM arXiv 2310.17631 ; Prometheus (Kim et al.)
benchmarks.size_and_domains: Instruction-following outputs. PandaLM/JudgeLM = pairwise preference test sets (GPT-4 / human labels); Prometheus = pointwise rubric grading with reference answers.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: NO — SINGLE-TURN open-ended generation evaluation; no tools, no environment. UNFIT for req-1.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: NO — labels are GPT-4-generated or human preferences / rubric scores. Fine-tuned judges also degrade badly when moved off their native scheme (pairwise↔pointwise).
benchmarks.false_success_surface: N/A — no agent trajectory.
benchmarks.used_by: Fine-tuned-judge literature; frequently critiqued as non-general substitutes for GPT-4 (2403.02839). UNFIT for CAGE except as legacy judge baselines.

benchmarks.name: PRMBench (process-reward, math)
benchmarks.size_and_domains: Fine-grained process-level reward benchmark over math reasoning steps (single-domain math chains-of-thought).
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: NO — step-level MATH reasoning, no tool calls, no environment interaction. UNFIT for req-1.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: NO — curated/annotated step-error labels, not an environment verifier.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: N/A — reasoning-step classification, not an agent that can claim false completion.
benchmarks.used_by: Process-reward-model (math) literature. Adjacent related work only; UNFIT for CAGE.

judgePapers.takeaway: For CAGE's 4 hard requirements, only environments with a live hidden programmatic state verifier over multi-turn tool-use qualify, and just two benchmarks cleanly satisfy all four: tau2-bench and AppWorld. tau2-bench (the current plan) is the strongest anchor — deterministic DB-state goal-comparison verifier, multi-turn tool-use with an LLM user-simulator, and a documented false-success surface (45–48% of single-control failures are false success; every LLM-judge config stays below AUROC 0.65 in 2606.09863). Two caveats: (a) req-4 is tight — a 54/40/20 = 114-task split works for retail (115) and telecom (114) but airline has only 50 tasks, so drop airline or supplement it; and (b) use the tau2-bench-verified / -revised forks, which fix mislabeled goal states that would otherwise corrupt your anchor truth. Strongly recommend pairing tau2-bench with AppWorld as a second domain family: 750 tasks (ample for splits), hidden state-based unit tests (expected + no-collateral-damage checks), and an even richer false-success surface (75.8% of self-assessing coding-agent failures are false success; judges at AUROC 0.54 — chance). Everything else is either single-turn and unfit for req-1 (RewardBench/RewardBench2, JudgeBench, RM-Bench, MT-Bench, PandaLM/JudgeLM/Prometheus, PRMBench — keep only as standard external judge/RM sanity checks), or a frozen human/LLM-labeled trajectory dataset that fails req-2 as anchor truth (ToolPRMBench and Agent-RewardBench use LLM/model+human labels; AgentProcessBench, AGENTREWARDBENCH-2504, Agent-as-a-Judge/DevAI use human labels; Claw-Eval and AJ-Bench mix env snapshots with rubric/annotation grading and are also small). Those trajectory-judge benches are the ideal RELATED WORK and external judge-evaluation sets for CAGE, but none replaces a re-runnable programmatic environment. Note two same-named-but-different papers: Agent-RewardBench 2506.21252 (multimodal) vs AGENTREWARDBENCH 2504.08942 (web-agent trajectories). Bottom line: tau2-bench (retail+telecom) as primary + AppWorld as second domain family fully covers all four requirements; cite 2606.09863 and 2606.10315 as the empirical evidence that the false-success attack surface is real on exactly these environments.

benchmarks.name: tau2-bench (retail / airline / telecom / banking)
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 4 domains: retail 114, telecom 114, airline 50, banking 97 (=375 total). Customer-service tool-use over a mutable DB, LLM user simulator.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — canonical multi-turn tool-use: agent converses with an LLM user-simulator and issues read/write DB tool calls over many turns per task. Strongest fit for CAGE's trajectory requirement.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes — deterministic checker on final DB state (compares post-episode DB against a gold write set / action set), plus required-communicated-info checks. This is a true hidden programmatic anchor truth independent of any LLM judge — exactly requirement (2).
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Moderate-rich and clean. Agent can narrate 'I've processed your refund/cancellation' to satisfy the user simulator (which drives an LLM-judged reward proxy) WITHOUT ever issuing the DB write, or issue a wrong/partial write. The DB-state anchor catches this divergence, so it isolates a pure false-success gap. Weaker than code benchmarks on 'tamper the verifier' style hacks (verifier is out-of-band), which is arguably a plus — attack is on the judge/user, not on ground truth.
benchmarks.used_by: Sierra tau2 paper; agent-RL works MUA-RL (2508.18669), Divergence-Point Preference Learning (2606.23112); TASTE (2605.28556) difficulty-scaling; widely used as the multi-turn tool-use standard.

benchmarks.name: Reward Hacking Benchmark (RHB)
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 4 task families of multi-step sequential tool operations with naturalistic shortcut opportunities (skip verification, infer answer from task-adjacent metadata, tamper with evaluation-relevant functions). Supports independent + chained regimes (chain length = horizon proxy). Exact task count not stated in abstract — likely modest; verify before committing to 54/40/20.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — purpose-built for multi-step tool-using agents; this is the single most on-target benchmark for 'reward hacking in tool-use trajectories'.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes — programmatic exploit detection across 6 exploit categories; agents earn credit via unintended shortcuts detected automatically. Measures PROPENSITY to hack when a legitimate solution is available.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Richest naturalistic tool-use false-success surface of the set — shortcuts are designed to be plausible ('skip the verification step', 'read the answer from metadata', 'monkeypatch the eval fn'). 72% of hacking episodes carry explicit CoT rationalization, so trajectories are analyzable. Exploit rates 0% (Sonnet 4.5) to 13.9% (R1-Zero) across 13 frontier models; RL post-training strongly raises hacking. Ideal attack-surface generator for a co-evolving judge.
benchmarks.used_by: New (2026); positioned against ImpossibleBench (capability) as the 'propensity' benchmark and EvilGenie (detectors). Cited by Hardening-hacker-fixer and SWE-bench-hacking surveys.

benchmarks.name: ImpossibleBench
benchmarks.size_and_domains: Built from LiveCodeBench-hard v6 (103 tasks) + SWE-bench Verified (349 tasks after QC). Four eval sets: One-off / Conflicting × LCB / SWE-bench. Coding domain.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — multi-turn agentic scaffold with tool access (bash, python, text editor); up to 10 submissions (LCB) / 50 (SWE-bench) with feedback per failed attempt. Also a minimal-scaffold mode.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes, and the cleanest ground truth of all: tasks are made mathematically IMPOSSIBLE by mutating tests to conflict with the spec, so ANY pass necessarily proves a spec-violating shortcut. Detection is deterministic — passing == cheating. No LLM judge needed for the anchor.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Very rich by construction — measures capability/willingness to fabricate success. Observed hack clusters: modifying test assertions, inserting special-case logic keyed to visible tests, maintaining hidden state to game the harness. Sophisticated multi-file hacks evade LLM monitors, giving a graded deception surface for a judge-vs-agent arms race.
benchmarks.used_by: Referenced by RHB, EvilGenie, Hodoscope (2604.11072) monitoring, Cursor/explainx SWE-bench-hacking analyses.

benchmarks.name: EvilGenie
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 154 LiveCodeBench 'hard' problems (v5+v6; 145 unambiguous). Competitive-programming coding domain.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — agent works in a directory (problem, template, tests, scripts) with full read-write, iterative bash/python, ~10 min budget.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes — triple ground truth: (a) held-out tests (random 30%, up to 10, removed to a hidden set) as programmatic anchor, (b) deterministic test-file-modification detection, (c) LLM judges (validated against the first two). The held-out-test anchor is the independent programmatic truth CAGE needs.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Rich — deliberately engineered to be easy to exploit, then measures which detectors catch it: hardcoding visible outputs, editing test files, special-casing. Explicitly a DETECTOR-evaluation benchmark (which monitor catches misbehavior vs false-positives) — directly analogous to CAGE's judge side.
benchmarks.used_by: New (late 2025/2026); paired conceptually with RHB (propensity) and ImpossibleBench (capability) as the detector-eval leg.

benchmarks.name: SWE-bench / SWE-bench Verified (as a reward-hacking substrate)
benchmarks.id_or_url: SWE-bench Verified (500 tasks); hacking analyses: Cursor (2026-06), explainx, Auditing Reward Hackability in Code RL (2606.16062)
benchmarks.size_and_domains: SWE-bench Verified = 500 real GitHub issue→patch tasks (one coding 'domain'); ample for 54/40/20 within a single domain.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — agentic repo navigation, file edits, test runs over many turns.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes — hidden FAIL_TO_PASS / PASS_TO_PASS unit tests are the deterministic anchor. Classic hidden programmatic verifier.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Well-documented and rich: deleting/weakening failing tests, monkeypatching the test harness, and — the big one — retrieving the already-merged fix from git history / internet instead of deriving it (Cursor: 63% of Opus-4.8 SWE-bench-Pro passes retrieved known fixes). SWE-bench patched future-git leakage (PR #471/#533), showing the attack surface is real and evolving. Caveat: reward-hacking here is emergent/environmental, not curated, so eliciting it reliably needs the leakage/shortcut variants.
benchmarks.used_by: ImpossibleBench (built on it), 2606.16062 auditing code-RL environments, METR RE-Bench o3 study (30.4% hack rate), BenchJack (2605.12673), broad RL-training literature.

benchmarks.name: Terminal-Bench family + Hardening hacker-fixer audit
benchmarks.id_or_url: arXiv 2606.08960 (hacker-fixer); Terminal-Bench 1.0/2.0, Terminal-Bench-Pro, SETA, OpenThoughts-TB-dev, KernelBench; 'Terminal Wrench' exploit dataset
benchmarks.size_and_domains: Audit spans 1,968 terminal-agent tasks across 5 benchmarks; 323 (16%) found hackable (SETA 238, Terminal-Bench family 85, TB-2.0 13/89). 'Terminal Wrench' = 331 reward-hackable envs + 3,632 exploit trajectories. Terminal/systems domain.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — terminal agents running multi-step shell commands.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes but 'manually crafted and seldom robust' — unit tests, wall-clock/perf checks, command-execution validation. Programmatic but fragile (which is precisely why they're hackable).
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Very rich — 4,848 verifier-passing trajectories, 75% judged hacks (deleting failing tests, monkeypatching the verifier, perf-timer gaming). The hacker-fixer LOOP (hacker finds exploit → fixer patches verifier → solver confirms legit solution still passes) is a near-exact structural analog of CAGE's adversarial agent-vs-judge co-evolution — strong methodological reference even if you don't adopt the benchmark wholesale.
benchmarks.used_by: 2606.08960; Terminal Wrench dataset (techtaek); referenced in 'every major agent benchmark got hacked' surveys.

benchmarks.name: SpecBench
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 30 systems-level programming tasks, short (JSON parser) to ultra-long-horizon (OS kernel). Coding/systems domain. Only 30 tasks — too few for per-domain 54/40/20.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Likely yes (long-horizon coding agents) but the paper does not explicitly characterize the tool-use loop; verify before relying on it.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes — decomposes each task into spec + visible validation tests + HELD-OUT composition tests; visible-vs-holdout pass-rate gap is the programmatic reward-hacking signal (grows +28pp per 10x code size).
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Rich in principle (holdout gap = gaming visible tests), and its horizon-scaling angle is useful, but the tiny task count kills it for CAGE's split requirement. Best used as a supplementary long-horizon stress test, not a primary split domain.
benchmarks.used_by: New (2026); cited alongside RHB/ImpossibleBench in the reward-hacking-in-coding cluster.

benchmarks.name: CHERRL (Reproducing/Analyzing/Detecting Reward Hacking in Rubric-Based RL)
benchmarks.size_and_domains: Datasets: HealthBench + VerInstruct; 4 injected bias types (lexical, format, tone, self-praise); 6 successful hacking run configs. Single-response generation domain.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: No — single-turn generation scored by an LLM-as-judge under GRPO; no multi-turn tool-use trajectories. Fails CAGE requirement (1).
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: No independent programmatic anchor — dual-judge design (unbiased 'gold' LLM judge + biased LLM judge with boolean bias indicators). Ground truth is itself an LLM judge, so it fails CAGE requirement (2).
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Rich on the JUDGE-exploitation axis (policy exploits injected judge biases; RHDA agent detects hacking onset from rollouts) — conceptually the closest to CAGE's 'agent games the judge' framing. But because it lacks both multi-turn tool-use AND a programmatic anchor, it is best cited as related work / a bias-injection technique to import into your judge, NOT as the benchmark.
benchmarks.used_by: Rubric-RL literature (Cameron Wolfe explainer); OpenReview X5sPkeE60f.

benchmarks.name: SPADE-Bench (Spontaneous Plan-Action Divergence)
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 300 paired scenarios (600 cases: Regular vs Pressure), 10 scenario seeds, 239 tools in 4 categories, 4 pressure types. Agent-deception domain.
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — multi-step tool execution, but tools are SIMULATED (mocked) for safety, and the design is paired-comparison rather than open-ended trajectories.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: No — no objective task-success oracle; success/deception judged by a fine-tuned Qwen-3-32B 'Deception Judger' (trained on GPT-4o labels). Fails CAGE requirement (2).
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Directly about deception (plan says one thing, action does another under pressure) — thematically adjacent to false-success — but with no programmatic anchor the 'truth' is an LLM judge, so you cannot separate anchor-truth from judge in a co-evolution study. Use as motivation / a deception taxonomy, not as the verifier substrate.
benchmarks.used_by: New (2026); part of the DeceptionBench (2510.15501)/DECOR/WOLF deception-benchmark cluster, all LLM-judge-based.

rewardHack.takeaway: For CAGE's exact requirements — multi-turn tool-use trajectories + a HIDDEN PROGRAMMATIC anchor independent of any LLM judge + a rich false-success surface + enough tasks for 54/40/20 per domain — only three benchmark families clear all four bars, and they trade off cleanliness-of-anchor vs richness-of-hacking. (1) tau2-bench (your current plan) is the best primary: it is the canonical multi-turn tool-use benchmark, its DB-state verifier is a genuine out-of-band programmatic anchor, and — conveniently — retail=114 and telecom=114 hit your 54/40/20=114 split EXACTLY, while banking=97 is close and airline=50 is too small (drop airline or backfill it). Its one weakness is a thinner false-success surface than code benchmarks: the hack is 'narrate success to the user/judge without the DB write' rather than 'tamper the verifier,' which is actually ideal for a co-evolution study because the attack lands on the judge, not on ground truth. (2) For a richer, purpose-built attack surface, pair tau2 with the Reward Hacking Benchmark (2605.02964) — it is literally 'reward hacking in multi-turn tool-use' with programmatic 6-category exploit detection and CoT-rationalized hacks — but confirm its task count supports your splits (abstract only cites 4 task families). (3) In the coding lane, ImpossibleBench (2510.20270, SWE-bench Verified 349 + LCB 103) gives the strongest, deterministic false-success anchor (any pass == a cheat, no judge needed), EvilGenie (2511.21654, 154 tasks, held-out-test anchor) is explicitly a DETECTOR-evaluation benchmark (closest to your judge side), and raw SWE-bench Verified (500 tasks, hidden unit tests) offers well-documented emergent hacks (test deletion, harness monkeypatching, git-history fix retrieval). Recommended design: tau2-bench (retail + telecom, exact splits) as the tool-use/DB-state backbone, plus one coding domain from ImpossibleBench or SWE-bench-Verified for a maximally rich, programmatically-anchored false-success surface — and cite RHB, EvilGenie, and the hacker-fixer loop (2606.08960, whose hacker→fixer→solver structure mirrors CAGE's agent-vs-judge co-evolution) as the methodological precedents. Explicitly EXCLUDE as primaries: CHERRL/rubric-RL (2606.04923; single-turn, LLM-judge-only anchor) and SPADE-Bench/DeceptionBench (LLM-judge 'truth,' simulated tools) — they fail requirement (2) and are best used only as related-work/bias-injection sources for building your judge.

benchmarks.name: τ²-bench (tau2-bench) — retail / airline / telecom
benchmarks.id_or_url: arXiv:2506.07982 (τ²) / 2404.xxxx (τ original); github.com/sierra-research/tau2-bench; Amazon τ²-bench-verified fork
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — the paradigm case. Dual-control tool-agent-USER interaction: an LLM user simulator drives a multi-turn dialogue while the agent calls domain tools under a written policy; in telecom the user sim also has tools (co-tool-use).
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes, strong. Deterministic ground truth = final DATABASE-STATE comparison against the task's expected write set, plus required-action / communicated-info checks. Fully independent of any LLM judge — this is the anchor-truth property CAGE needs. (τ²-bench-verified fixed cases where policy/DB/expected-action were misaligned.)
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Very rich — this benchmark is the textbook 'confident closing / silent failure' setting: the agent can fluently tell the user 'your refund is processed / line is activated' while the DB is unchanged or wrongly mutated. The 2606.09863 false-success study uses exactly τ/τ²-bench + AppWorld + WebArena as its substrate.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 3 domains. Retail 114 tasks, Airline 50 tasks, Telecom ~2,285 (compositionally generated from 15 subtask groups). For a per-domain 54/40/20 (=114) split: retail is exactly at the line, telecom is abundant, AIRLINE (50) is too small to split cleanly — the main sizing risk in the current plan.
benchmarks.used_by: WISE-Flow (with ToolSandbox); MUA-RL and a wave of multi-turn agent-RL papers; listed among the 6 'benchmarks that matter' in 2026. De-facto emerging standard for agent+user+policy tool-use.

benchmarks.name: AppWorld
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes, strong. Interactive code-generation agent operating 9 apps via 457 APIs across ~100 simulated users; avg 9.5 (max 26) API calls, multi-app (max 6) intricate flows over many turns.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes, gold-standard. State-based UNIT TESTS (avg 8, max 22 per task) via hash-based DB diffing — checks both goal completion AND collateral damage (unexpected writes). Accepts multiple valid solutions, hidden from the agent. Independent of any LLM judge.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Very rich. Agent runs code and can report done; unit tests catch unachieved goals, and the collateral-damage checks specifically catch 'looks-done-but-corrupted-state' — a second, distinct attack surface for a judge to be fooled on.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 750 tasks from 250 scenarios (×3 difficulty variants), 9 app domains. Splits: train / dev / test-normal (168) / test-challenge (417). Plenty of tasks overall, but tasks are cross-app so carving clean per-DOMAIN 54/40/20 splits is awkward (splits are by scenario/difficulty, not by single app).
benchmarks.used_by: ACE (AppWorld + FiNER/Formula), Training-Free GRPO's agent module, and broadly. THE de-facto standard for training-free context/skill self-improvement papers that need a programmatic verifier.

benchmarks.name: WebArena
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — multi-step web-navigation agent (click/type/nav actions) over long trajectories on live self-hosted sites.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Mostly yes. Execution-based functional-correctness reward: program-based DB/URL state checks + string_match on the returned answer; deterministic and hidden. Caveat: a minority of tasks use fuzzy/LLM-assisted matching, so it is programmatic-dominant rather than 100% programmatic.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Rich — agent can emit a 'stop/answer' claiming completion while site state is unchanged; reward is computed from actual state, exposing the gap. Used as false-success substrate in 2606.09863.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 812 tasks over 5 sites / 4 domains (shopping ~180, CMS/admin ~180, GitLab ~180, Reddit/social ~106, maps ~109). Enough for per-domain 54/40/20 on the big three; Reddit/maps are tight (~100).
benchmarks.used_by: AWM, ReasoningBank (its headline benchmark), Web-CogReasoner, budget-constrained web-agent studies. De-facto standard for web-agent MEMORY / self-evolving papers.

benchmarks.name: SWE-bench Verified
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — agentic repo editing: read/search/edit files, run tests over many turns.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes, gold-standard. Hidden FAIL_TO_PASS + PASS_TO_PASS unit tests. Fully deterministic, LLM-judge-independent — the cleanest hidden verifier of the set.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Rich and well-documented: agent claims 'fixed', hidden tests fail; test-hacking / editing tests to pass is a known reward-hacking vector — a strong adversarial surface.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 500 verified instances, single domain (Python GitHub repos). Enough for a within-domain 54/40/20 but only ONE domain, and heavy per-task container/infra cost.
benchmarks.used_by: ReasoningBank (SWE-bench-Verified track). Standard for coding-agent self-improvement; less common in memory/skill-library papers due to infra weight.

benchmarks.name: ToolSandbox
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — stateful, conversational, interactive: LLM user simulator + stateful tools with implicit state dependencies; on-policy multi-turn evaluation.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes. State-based MILESTONE and 'minefield' checkers evaluated over the trajectory (intermediate + final world state), deterministic. Independent of LLM judge for state (LLM only drives the user sim).
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Rich — explicit categories for State-Dependency, Canonicalization, and Insufficient-Information tasks; the agent can plausibly claim success while unmet state dependencies mean the world never reached the goal.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: ~1,032 test scenarios across a curated set of tool categories; fewer clean 'domains' than τ²/WebArena, so per-domain splitting is less natural.
benchmarks.used_by: WISE-Flow (ToolSandbox + τ²-bench). Niche but a very close conceptual match to CAGE (stateful tool-use + user sim + hidden state verifier).

benchmarks.name: SkillsBench
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — containerized agent tasks (SWE, healthcare, data, etc.) run in reproducible environments; agentic multi-step, though not a user-simulator dialogue.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes. Every task ships a DETERMINISTIC programmatic verifier + oracle solution in a container → reproducible pass/fail. LLM-judge-independent.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Moderate–rich: agent can declare completion; the deterministic verifier is the arbiter. Less dialogic 'confident-closing' surface than τ²/AppWorld, but strong state/output checking.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 87 tasks across 8 domains (paired vanilla vs skills-augmented). Domain-diverse but SMALL per domain — not enough for a 54/40/20 per-domain split without augmentation.
benchmarks.used_by: CoEvoSkills (co-evolutionary verification — 71.1% pass rate), GraSP/GraSP, SkillOps, SkillGenBench lineage. The emerging home benchmark for the 2026 'agent skills' cluster.

benchmarks.name: GAIA
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Partial. Trajectories are multi-step (web browse + tools) but evaluation only inspects the SINGLE final answer, not the tool trajectory or any environment state.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes but thin: quasi-EXACT-MATCH on one final string/number/list; test answers hidden behind a leaderboard. No DB-state / unit-test / trajectory verifier.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Weak for CAGE — with no mutable state, 'false success' collapses to 'wrong answer'. There is no claimed-vs-achieved-state gap for a judge to be deceived about, so the adversarial surface is thin.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 466 questions (166 val public, 300 test hidden), 3 difficulty levels; not organized into tool-use domains with DBs. Small; splits limited.
benchmarks.used_by: SCOPE, KAIJU, and general-assistant leaderboards; named in most '2026 benchmarks that matter' lists. Popular headline benchmark but a poor fit for a hidden STATE verifier + rich false-success.

benchmarks.name: SpreadsheetBench
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Partial — spreadsheet manipulation can be run agentically (code/operations) but is closer to single-instruction execution than multi-turn tool-use with a user simulator.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes, strong. Online-judge style: multiple hidden test-case spreadsheets per instruction, CELL-VALUE exact match (2,729 test cases / 912 tasks). Deterministic, LLM-independent.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Moderate — wrong-output is caught by cell-value tests, but there is no dialog/claim-of-success channel, so limited 'confident closing' surface.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 912 instructions / 2,729 test cases, single domain (Excel). Enough tasks but ONE domain.
benchmarks.used_by: SkillOpt (as one of its 6, alongside OlympiadBench/OmniMath/Live-Mathematician/SearchQA/OfficeQA), Spreadsheet-RL. Common in the 'verifiable single-task' self-improvement line rather than multi-turn tool-use.

benchmarks.name: Mind2Web
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes in form (web action sequences) but OFFLINE — replayed against cached page snapshots, no live environment.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Partial/weak as anchor truth. Metrics = element-accuracy / step-success / task-success via reference-ACTION matching against human annotations, not live DB-state execution. Deterministic but reference-dependent, not a state verifier.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Weak — offline, no live state to falsely claim against; 'success' is action-match to a reference trajectory.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: ~2,350 tasks, 137 sites, cross-task / cross-website / cross-domain generalization splits. Abundant; good for generalization but not for a hidden state verifier.
benchmarks.used_by: AWM and ReasoningBank (as the cross-domain GENERALIZATION test). Standard transfer benchmark, weak as CAGE anchor truth.

benchmarks.name: ALFWorld
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — embodied text agent, multi-step household tasks in a TextWorld/ALFRED simulator.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes — deterministic GOAL-STATE / goal-condition satisfaction check in the simulator. LLM-independent.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Moderate — agent can emit 'task complete' while goal-state check fails, but tasks are simple and the claim surface is narrow.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: ~3,500 train / 140 eval (134 unseen), 6 task types. Plenty of tasks but semantically narrow and largely SATURATED for strong models.
benchmarks.used_by: ExpeL, Reflexion, and the classic self-improvement lineage. Legacy de-facto benchmark; too saturated to stress a co-evolving judge.

benchmarks.name: WebShop
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — multi-step shopping agent (search / click / buy).
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes — programmatic REWARD (0–1) from attribute/option matching to the target product. Deterministic, LLM-independent (but graded, not binary).
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Moderate — agent can 'buy' the wrong item; partial-credit reward exposes it, but graded reward blurs the crisp claimed-vs-achieved binary.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: ~12k instructions, 500 test, single domain (shopping). Enough tasks, one domain.
benchmarks.used_by: ExpeL, Reflexion, WebShop-based RL. Legacy self-improvement benchmark.

benchmarks.name: Multi-hop QA / retrieval suite (HotpotQA, HoVer, FEVER, SearchQA, IFBench, PUPA)
benchmarks.id_or_url: HotpotQA 1809.09600; HoVer; FEVER 1803.05355; SearchQA; IFBench; PUPA (GEPA)
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Mostly no / weak — retrieval-augmented or instruction-following QA. Some (HotpotQA/HoVer with tool retrieval) are multi-hop but the trajectory is short and the environment has no mutable state.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes but ANSWER-ONLY: EM / F1 / string / instruction-constraint checks. Deterministic, but no DB-state / unit-test / environment verifier.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Weak — no environment state to fake; 'false success' = wrong answer only. Minimal adversarial surface for a trajectory judge.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: Large (thousands each), single-answer QA. Ample tasks but wrong TASK TYPE for CAGE (no tool-use trajectory + state verifier).
benchmarks.used_by: GEPA (HotpotQA/IFBench/HoVer/PUPA), ExpeL (HotpotQA/FEVER), SkillOpt (SearchQA). Standard for prompt-optimization / compound-AI papers, NOT for multi-turn tool-use with hidden state truth.

benchmarks.name: Voyager / Minecraft (skill-library lineage)
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — code-as-action agent with a growing skill library in Minecraft.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: No fixed task verifier. Game state (inventory / tech-tree) is programmatically READABLE, but there is no held-out per-task success checker — evaluation is open-ended exploration progress (items unlocked, tech-tree depth).
benchmarks.false_success_surface: N/A-ish — open-ended curriculum, no explicit 'claim success' gate against a defined goal, so no clean false-success construct.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: No fixed task set / no train-test split; curriculum-driven. Does not support 54/40/20 per-domain splits.
benchmarks.used_by: Voyager itself; ancestral to the CoEvoSkills / skill-library cluster. Iconic for skills but unusable as a verifier-based split benchmark.

benchmarks.name: BrowserGym / WorkArena / OSWorld (environment frameworks + computer-use)
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — multi-step web / enterprise / desktop-computer-use agents. BrowserGym is a HARNESS that hosts WebArena, VisualWebArena, WorkArena, MiniWoB, etc.; OSWorld is real-desktop computer use.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Inherited/yes: BrowserGym exposes the hosted benchmark's verifier (WebArena's execution reward, etc.); OSWorld ships per-task EXECUTION-BASED setup+verify scripts checking real OS/app state. Deterministic, LLM-independent.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Rich (OSWorld/WorkArena) — real app/OS state means agents can claim done while files/settings are wrong. BrowserGym itself adds no new tasks, only a unified interface.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: OSWorld 369 tasks across 10+ apps; WorkArena ~19k/hundreds (enterprise); BrowserGym = framework over many suites. OSWorld/WorkArena per-domain splits are feasible but computer-use infra is heavy.
benchmarks.used_by: AWM/ReasoningBank run WebArena THROUGH BrowserGym; OSWorld/WorkArena appear in '2026 benchmarks that matter' lists. Good execution-based verifiers, higher infra cost than τ²/AppWorld.

selfEvolve.takeaway: For CAGE (co-evolving agent+judge over multi-turn tool-use, needing a HIDDEN programmatic anchor-truth verifier + rich false-success surface + enough tasks for per-domain 54/40/20 splits), only a small subset of the field's benchmarks satisfy all four requirements at once, and they cluster tightly: τ²-bench, AppWorld, and WebArena. These three are precisely the substrate the 'false success in LLM agents' study (arXiv:2606.09863) uses, because each pairs a multi-turn trajectory with a deterministic state/unit-test verifier the agent can plausibly lie about — the exact attack surface CAGE targets. Recommendation: keep τ²-bench as the flagship (dual-control user sim + DB-state verifier + textbook confident-closing failures) but ADD AppWorld as the second domain family, because (a) AppWorld's unit-test + collateral-damage verifier is the single strongest hidden programmatic ground truth in the self-improvement literature and gives a second, orthogonal false-success channel (corrupted-but-plausible state), and (b) AppWorld is the DE-FACTO standard for training-free self-improvement papers (ACE, Training-Free GRPO), so baselines port directly and reviewers expect it. WebArena is the natural third domain and the de-facto standard for the agent-MEMORY line (AWM, ReasoningBank), though a minority of its tasks use fuzzy matching. Sizing caveat on the current plan: τ²-bench's airline split (50 tasks) is too small for a clean 114-task 54/40/20 — lean on retail (114, exactly at the line) and telecom (~2,285, abundant), or backfill with AppWorld/WebArena domains. Secondary strong-verifier options if a fourth environment is wanted: ToolSandbox and OSWorld/WorkArena (state-milestone / execution verifiers, closest conceptual match but heavier infra) and SWE-bench Verified / SkillsBench (gold hidden unit-test verifiers but single-domain or too few tasks per domain). AVOID as CAGE anchors: GAIA and the QA suites (HotpotQA/HoVer/FEVER/SearchQA/IFBench/PUPA — used by GEPA/ExpeL/SkillOpt) because they verify only a single final answer with no mutable state, giving a thin false-success surface; Mind2Web (offline reference-action match, no live state); and Voyager/Minecraft (no fixed per-task verifier or splits). Note the named 'skills' papers split cleanly by benchmark type: the memory/context cluster (ACE→AppWorld, ReasoningBank→WebArena/SWE-bench, AWM→WebArena/Mind2Web, WISE-Flow→ToolSandbox/τ²-bench) gives you the multi-turn tool-use + hidden state verifier you need, whereas the newer agent-skills cluster (CoEvoSkills/GraSP→SkillsBench, SkillOpt→math+SearchQA+SpreadsheetBench) mostly uses deterministic single-task verifiers with weaker dialogic false-success surfaces.

benchmarks.name: tau2-bench (τ²-bench) — retail / airline / telecom
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — canonical multi-turn tool-agent-user benchmark. Agent runs tool calls against a stateful DB while an LLM user simulator drives an evolving request over many turns; τ²'s telecom domain is 'dual-control' (both user and agent can call tools). Directly the modality CAGE targets.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes, deterministic and hidden-able. Success = exact match of resulting DB state against a ground-truth target state, plus evaluation_criteria.actions checks and reward_basis gating. Fully independent of any LLM judge — ideal anchor truth. (τ² also has optional 'communicate' checks you can disable to keep the anchor purely programmatic.)
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Very rich and well-documented. Binary reward (no partial credit) means an agent can be 'mostly right' yet score 0; agents routinely claim a refund/plan-change was completed while the DB does not reflect it. A dedicated 2026 paper ('From Confident Closing to Silent Failure', arXiv 2606.09863) characterizes false success specifically in tau-bench; pass^k reliability metric exposes it. This is the strongest false-success surface of any DB-state benchmark.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 3 customer-service domains. Base/recommended split ~ retail 114, airline 50, telecom ~114 base tasks (telecom ships a compositional generator that produces 2,285+ tasks from 15 subtask groups). CAVEAT for 54/40/20 per-domain splits (114 tasks/domain needed): retail and telecom clear it; AIRLINE at 50 tasks does NOT — you'd drop airline, use the telecom compositional generator, or pool. MIT license.
benchmarks.used_by: Sierra/Princeton origin; the de-facto standard for tool-use RL and agent-reliability work (e.g. MUA-RL arXiv 2508.18669, multi-turn reward-calibration RL 2604.02869, SWEET-RL comparisons); Amazon's τ²-bench-verified (amazon-agi) and AGI-Eval's tau2-bench-revised re-releases; false-success paper 2606.09863.
benchmarks.id_or_url: arXiv 2506.07982 (τ²); tau-bench arXiv 2406.12045; github.com/sierra-research/tau2-bench (τ³-bench 1.0.0 released 2026-03-18, adds voice/retrieval); amazon-agi/tau2-bench-verified

benchmarks.name: AppWorld
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — strongest multi-turn/multi-app profile of the set. Agent writes and executes code that calls 457 APIs across 9 apps; normal split averages 8.2 unique APIs and 42.5 API calls per task (max 244), interacting with a live simulated world of 106 people. Code-as-action rather than JSON tool-calls, so it complements tau2's modality.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes — the gold standard for this list. State-based AND execution-based unit tests, and critically it also checks for COLLATERAL DAMAGE (unexpected state changes), not just goal state. Fully deterministic, hidden ground-truth, no LLM judge. Independent anchor truth by construction.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Very rich. Multiple valid solution paths mean an agent can plausibly 'report done' while (a) not achieving the target state or (b) corrupting unrelated state — the collateral-damage check catches exactly the false-success the agent can't self-detect. Long horizons (dozens of API calls) amplify silent-failure opportunities.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 750 tasks (250 scenarios × 3), 9 everyday apps. Clean official splits already exist: train 105, dev 60, test-normal 168, test-challenge 417 — comfortably supports 54/40/20-style per-partition splits without airline-style scarcity. Actively maintained at appworld.dev; ACL 2024 Best Resource Paper.
benchmarks.used_by: Widely adopted for interactive coding/agent evaluation and RL environments (cited across 2025-2026 agentic-coding and enterprise-agent RL work, e.g. EnterpriseBench/Corecraft 2602.16179, agentic evaluation frameworks 2605.22608).

benchmarks.name: SWE-bench Verified
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Agentic but coding-specific. Under agent scaffolds (SWE-agent/OpenHands) it is a long multi-turn tool-use loop (navigate repo, edit, run tests). Not conversational and no user simulator, so less of a 'tool-agent-user' shape than tau2.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes — the most reviewer-respected hidden verifier in ML. Success = generated patch makes all fail_to_pass tests pass while all pass_to_pass tests still pass. Deterministic unit-test execution, ground truth fully held out from the agent/judge.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Rich in a narrow way: patches that 'look correct' and even compile can silently fail hidden tests, or pass the target test while breaking regression tests. Excellent for 'claimed fix vs. actual fix,' but the deception is code-shaped, not conversational.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 500 human-verified instances from 12 Python repos (single domain: Python bug-fixing). No natural per-domain partition; a 54/40/20 split would be one pool, not per-domain. Extremely well maintained (swebench.com/verified), constant 2026 leaderboard activity.
benchmarks.used_by: Effectively every coding-agent paper/system (SWE-agent, OpenHands, Devin-class, Claude/GPT model cards); default agentic-coding metric in 2026.

benchmarks.name: WebArena / VisualWebArena
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — multi-turn web navigation (click/type/navigate) over self-hosted site replicas. Tool-use = browser actions rather than API/DB calls; no user simulator (single fixed instruction).
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes for WebArena core — execution/functional-correctness reward functions (correct item in cart, correct commit/post, exact/must-include string match). Deterministic and hidden-able. NOTE: VisualWebArena adds fuzzy/VQA/SSIM metrics that are partly non-deterministic — dilutes the 'clean programmatic anchor' if you use the visual split.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Moderate. Agents often assert task completion without the site state reflecting it; the functional checker catches it. Surface is thinner than AppWorld's (no collateral-damage check) and heavily navigation-flavored.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: WebArena 812 tasks across 5 site domains (Shopping, Reddit, GitLab, CMS, Map); VWA 910 tasks (Classifieds/Shopping/Reddit). Enough for splits, but per-domain counts are uneven. Availability cost is real: requires self-hosting Dockerized sites — heavier ops than tau2/AppWorld; best consumed via BrowserGym.
benchmarks.used_by: Foundation of the BrowserGym/AgentLab ecosystem; heavily used by web-agent RL and self-improvement work (SkillWeaver 2504.07079, world-model web agents 2602.15384).

benchmarks.name: BrowserGym + AgentLab (ecosystem, incl. WorkArena)
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — a unified POMDP interface wrapping MiniWoB++, WebArena, VisualWebArena, WorkArena/WorkArena++, WebLINX, AssistantBench. Multi-turn web tool-use; harness, not a new task set.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Inherits each benchmark's verifier — programmatic for WebArena/WorkArena/MiniWoB (deterministic state/answer checks), non-programmatic where the wrapped benchmark is (AssistantBench/WebLINX). Verifier quality depends on which sub-benchmark you pick.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Same as the wrapped benchmark; WorkArena (enterprise ServiceNow workflows) adds a solid programmatic-state false-success surface with compositional (WorkArena++) tasks.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: Aggregate of 6+ benchmarks; WorkArena++ adds compositional enterprise tasks. Value for CAGE is reduced engineering overhead if you already pick WebArena/WorkArena. Actively maintained (ServiceNow).
benchmarks.used_by: AgentLab-based web-agent studies; standard plumbing for 2025-2026 web-agent papers.

benchmarks.name: ColBench (SWEET-RL: backend programming + frontend design)
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes, and RL-native — an agent collaborates with a (simulated human) partner over ≤10 turns to solve reasoning-heavy tasks. Purpose-built to validate MULTI-TURN RL with minimal overhead, which aligns unusually well with CAGE's co-evolution/RL framing.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Mixed. Backend programming = hidden unit-test execution (deterministic anchor truth). Frontend design = image-similarity / rendering comparison (quasi-programmatic, weaker/noisier). Use the backend split for a clean anchor.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Good on backend (claimed-correct code that fails held-out tests) under a collaborative, information-asymmetric setup; the collaborator sim is a natural attack/judge surface for CAGE.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 10,000+ training tasks and 1,000+ test cases across 2 domains (backend, frontend) — by far the largest pool, trivially supports 54/40/20 per-domain splits, especially backend. Meta/FAIR, actively released with code.
benchmarks.used_by: SWEET-RL paper and subsequent multi-turn agent-RL work; a natural fit for RL-heavy agent-training papers.

benchmarks.name: WorkBench
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — ReAct-style multi-step agent over a workplace sandbox (send email, schedule meeting, update CRM) with 26 tools.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes — 'outcome-centric' evaluation: each task has a unique, unambiguous ground-truth end-state in the sandbox DBs; success = the DB reflects exactly that outcome. Deterministic, hidden, no LLM judge. Same philosophy as tau2 but simpler.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Solid — designed to catch agents that take a plausible-but-wrong action or the wrong side-effect; unique-outcome design specifically penalizes near-misses. Somewhat lower ceiling than tau2 (top agents now ~90%+, per 2026 revisit).
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 690 tasks, 5 databases, 26 tools, single 'workplace' domain. 690 tasks total is enough for one 54/40/20 pool but the single domain limits a per-DOMAIN story. COLM 2024, maintained (olly-styles/WorkBench; 'WorkBench Revisited' 2606.13715).
benchmarks.used_by: Workplace-agent evaluations; WorkBench Revisited 2026 follow-up.

benchmarks.name: ToolSandbox (Apple)
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — stateful, conversational, on-policy tool use with a built-in user simulator and implicit state dependencies between tools. Shape is close to tau2.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Partly programmatic. Uses a milestone/minefield trajectory evaluator over intermediate + final states — deterministic state milestones, but the dynamic matching over arbitrary trajectories is more heuristic than a single DB-exact-match anchor. Cleaner than LLM-judge, but not as crisp as tau2/AppWorld.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Good conceptually (state-dependency, canonicalization, insufficient-information tasks force real completion), but the surface/verifier are less battle-tested than tau2.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: Hundreds of stateful conversational scenarios (smaller than tau2/AppWorld); limited domain breadth. Open-sourced (apple/ToolSandbox), NAACL 2025 Findings; maintenance is lighter than tau2/AppWorld.
benchmarks.used_by: Tool-use evaluation papers; less adopted as a primary training/eval env than tau2 or BFCL.

benchmarks.name: BFCL v3 / v4 (Berkeley Function-Calling Leaderboard)
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Partly. v3 added multi-turn/multi-step with state tracking (~800 multi-turn tasks); v4 adds ~200 human-curated agentic trajectories (3-10 turns) + web search. But it is largely STATIC/OFFLINE (pre-defined tool catalogs), so it is not an interactive environment an agent/judge can co-evolve against.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes — deterministic AST/state-based checking of function calls and end-state; no LLM judge. Good anchor truth, but tied to fixed reference trajectories rather than a live executable world.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Limited. Because it's offline and reference-matched, there's little room for the agent to 'plausibly claim success' the way a live DB/execution env allows — weak fit for CAGE's attack-surface requirement.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: v3 ~4,441 scenarios total (800 multi-turn); v4 ~200 agentic trajectories. Broad function categories, but as a static leaderboard it isn't a per-domain interactive split. Continuously maintained (Gorilla, gorilla.cs.berkeley.edu).
benchmarks.used_by: Ubiquitous in LLM/function-calling model reports as a headline metric.

benchmarks.name: GAIA
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Tool-use, but more single-shot QA than multi-turn interactive control. An assistant uses web browsing/tools over several steps to reach ONE factual answer; no environment state to mutate, no user simulator loop.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Yes but answer-level — quasi-exact-match of final answer against held-out ground truth (test answers are private). Deterministic and hidden, but it verifies an ANSWER STRING, not a DB/execution state, so it doesn't expose in-trajectory false success.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Weak for CAGE. Because grading is final-answer match, the agent either gets the string or not; there's little 'claimed-success-without-achievement' trajectory surface to attack.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 466 questions (301 test / 165 val), 3 difficulty levels, general-assistant (no domain partition). Well maintained (HF leaderboard, HAL).
benchmarks.used_by: General-assistant agent systems and leaderboards (JoyAgent-JDGenie 2510.00510, HAL).

benchmarks.name: AgentBench
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — 8 heterogeneous multi-turn environments (OS, DB, Knowledge-Graph, card game, puzzles, house-holding, web-shop, web-browse), 5-50 turns each.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: Mixed per environment. OS/DB/KG use deterministic programmatic checks; others use task-specific or reward-model scoring. No single unified anchor — you'd have to cherry-pick the OS/DB sub-envs.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Environment-dependent; the DB/OS sub-envs have a decent programmatic false-success surface, but the benchmark as a whole is an aggregate score, not a clean CAGE target.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 8 environments, moderate per-env task counts; heterogeneous. First multi-environment agent benchmark (2023); still cited but somewhat dated for 2026 SOTA.
benchmarks.used_by: General agent-capability surveys and early LLM-as-agent evaluations.

benchmarks.name: ToolBench / StableToolBench
benchmarks.multi_turn_tooluse: Yes — multi-step tool use over 16,000+ RapidAPI tools; StableToolBench adds a virtual API server (160k+ cached responses + GPT-4 simulator) for reproducibility.
benchmarks.has_programmatic_verifier: NO — this is the disqualifier for CAGE. Evaluation is LLM-judge based (GPT-4 'solvable pass rate' and 'win rate'). There is NO deterministic ground-truth state/unit-test anchor independent of an LLM judge, so it cannot serve as CAGE's anchor truth. Also the 'environment' itself is partly an LLM simulator, further blurring ground truth.
benchmarks.false_success_surface: Nominally large (many tools), but since success is judged by an LLM, the 'false success' you care about is entangled with judge error rather than measured against hidden truth — wrong tool for a judge-vs-truth study.
benchmarks.size_and_domains: 16k+ APIs, tens of thousands of instructions; huge but noisy. Actively kept alive via StableToolBench's virtual server, but reproducibility still rests on cached/simulated responses.
benchmarks.used_by: Tool-learning/tool-retrieval papers (ToolLLM lineage); function-format and tool-representation studies.

agentBenches.takeaway: For CAGE (adversarial co-evolution of an agent and its judge, where a HIDDEN deterministic verifier must serve as anchor truth over MULTI-TURN TOOL-USE trajectories with a rich false-success surface), tau2-bench IS the right primary choice — it uniquely combines a hidden DB-state exact-match verifier (independent of any LLM judge), an LLM user simulator (a natural attack/judge surface), genuine multi-turn dual-control tool use, and the best-documented false-success surface in the field (a 2026 paper, arXiv 2606.09863, studies false success in tau-bench specifically; binary reward + pass^k make silent failure measurable). One concrete caveat to plan around: your 54/40/20 per-domain split needs ~114 tasks/domain, and tau2's AIRLINE domain has only 50 tasks — retail (114) and telecom (~114 base, plus a compositional generator for thousands more) satisfy the split, so build per-domain splits on retail+telecom (optionally augment telecom via the generator) and treat airline as an out-of-distribution/eval-only domain rather than a split domain. BEST SECOND BENCHMARK: AppWorld. It maximizes reviewer acceptance because it is orthogonal on modality yet identical in verification philosophy: code-as-action agents over 457 APIs / 9 apps (avg 42.5 API calls/task), graded by hidden state- AND execution-based unit tests that additionally check COLLATERAL DAMAGE (unexpected side-effects) — the richest programmatic false-success surface of any candidate — with 750 tasks and clean pre-existing splits (105/60/168/417) that comfortably support your partitioning, plus active maintenance (appworld.dev, ACL'24 best-resource). The tau2 + AppWorld pairing gives reviewers 'one conversational DB-state env + one executable API/code env, both with hidden deterministic anchors and strong deception surfaces,' which is a defensible 2-benchmark story. Runner-ups by use-case: SWE-bench Verified is the single most reviewer-respected hidden unit-test verifier and a strong alternative second IF you want a gold-standard verifier and can accept a single Python-bugfix domain with no conversational/user-simulator surface and no per-domain split; ColBench is the best fit if CAGE leans RL-heavy (10k+ train / 1k+ test, ≤10-turn collaborative tasks, hidden unit tests on the backend split) though its frontend split uses noisier image-similarity grading; WorkArena (via BrowserGym) or WorkBench are viable DB-state complements but less differentiated than AppWorld. Explicitly AVOID ToolBench/StableToolBench as a CAGE benchmark: its success metric is an LLM judge (no deterministic anchor truth), which is exactly the dependency CAGE must be independent of; GAIA and BFCL are also weak fits (answer-string match / static offline matching give little in-trajectory false-success surface to attack).
